input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10th‑scale autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technologies for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) testing. The vehicle comprises a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and autonomy elements—including ACC logic, sensor suites, and actuators—which together enable teleoperated control and real‑time state data collection. Its compact, controlled‑track design facilitates rapid prototyping, high sensor density, and efficient integration with digital twin services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Three core services are provided: (1) Data Monitoring – a web‑based UI that displays live vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate telemetry reports; (2) Computation Offloading – the Acceleration Control (ACC) algorithms are migrated from on‑board ECU to a cloud or edge server, which calculates desired speed based on lead vehicle distance and returns the command to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands from a remote location via low end‑to‑end latency communication. These services target operators (for monitoring and remote control) and developers (for troubleshooting and data analysis).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on cloud‑based compute resources for ACC logic off‑loading, low‑latency bidirectional communication (e.g., WebSocket/MQTT), and a web‑based user interface to visualize telemetry, manage ACC states, and produce data reports. The Digital Twin System (DTS) bundles the DT Entity, physical twin, middleware components, and UI, providing a unified platform for data collection, real‑time visualization, and remote operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Computational offloading requires all automotive edge devices to share a common notion of time; safety‑critical vehicular standards mandate clock synchronization precision tighter than 100 ms, but due to hardware limits the system enforces a 100 ms synchronization tolerance. This 100 ms bound defines the temporal granularity of the virtual model, establishing the maximum allowable latency between timestamps on physical sensor data and the corresponding digital representation and constraining the update frequency for time‑dependent services across the twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture, allocating the application into three distinct Functional Entities (FE): the Physical Twin Entity, the Digital Twin Entity, and the User Entity. Each FE is implemented as a separate ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. The User Entity hosts a single User Interface FE that is split into a 3 D Visualization FE and a Control Panel FE. The Digital Twin Entity contains FE modules for data storage, data management, and control logic, while the Physical Twin Entity aggregates sensor, actuator, and compute resources and hosts automotive‑specific services such as Point Cloud Clustering and Tracking. Deviations from the ISO 23247 standard are noted, particularly the introduction of the Physical Twin Entity, influencing ownership and engineering boundaries. All entities are physically deployed on separate hardware units within the vehicle, and coordination among them is achieved through ROS 2 communication mechanisms (publish/subscribe, services, actions) and higher‑level orchestration handled by the ISO 23247 actors.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data originating from the physical twin includes raw LiDAR point‑cloud samples, vehicle state variables, and ACC‑relevant telemetry. These streams are transmitted to the Digital Twin platform over a Wi‑Fi link employing UDP. Within the Digital Twin, the data is published as ROS 2 topics; a Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component performs spatial segmentation and 2D projection of the raw point cloud. The ISO 23247‑based Data Translation functional element then serialises the data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions before it is fed into the core processing services.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Digital Twin—such as motor actuation signals and steering servo commands—are formatted as ROS 2 service calls or topic messages and transmitted back to the vehicle’s on‑board actuators via the same Wi‑Fi UDP network. The command payloads are serialised by the ISO 23247 Data Translation component to ensure proper unit representation. Feedback from the actuators is returned through the same communication channel, allowing the Digital Twin to confirm execution and resolve any conflicts, although specific conflict‑resolution logic is not described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin services communicate over a ROS 2 network, with internal component interaction using ROS 2 topics and services. Inter‑entity communication—between the physical twin’s on‑board hardware and the Digital Twin—occurs over a Wi‑Fi radio link using UDP in the transport layer. All data is serialised through the ISO 23247 Data Translation functional element to maintain a consistent format. The document does not specify bandwidth, latency, reliability, or security details beyond the use of UDP.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Digital Twin system follows ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing, adopting its reference architecture which defines an entity‑based structure. Implementation of Functional Entities may use OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as a concrete technology. The system aligns with interoperability requirements outlined in ISO 23247 Part 2, ensuring standardized data exchange, device control, and integration across manufacturing elements.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The digital twin system is grounded in ISO 26262‑1:2018 functional‑safety requirements and the ISO 15622‑2018 performance test procedures for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). It follows the ISO 26262 safety lifecycle, comprising concept, development, production, operation and decommission phases, with explicit Safety‑Integrity Levels (SIL 1–4) defined for each safety‑critical functionality. Hazard Analysing, Risk Assessment (HARA) and Fault‑Tree/Fault‑Mode & Effect Analysis (FMECA) are mandated to derive safety goals, safety requirements and corresponding SIL assignments. Failure‑mode testing is carried out per ISO 15622: ACC must maintain a safe following distance of 3 m at 45 km/h and use a fail‑safe mode that brings the vehicle to a controlled stop or initiates a hard‑brake state if a critical sensor fault is detected.

For V2X communications the system implements the 3GPP TS 22.185 v15.0.0 Release 15 security architecture. Authentication is performed via a Public‑Key Infrastructure (PKI) with X.509 certificates issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA). Mutual TLS (or DTLS) is used to establish an encrypted channel, leveraging AES‑256 in Galois/Counter Mode (GCM). Message integrity is guaranteed with ECDSA signatures (256‑bit group, prime field). The system supports pseudonym‑based privacy protection, rotating pseudonym certificates every 60 s to limit tracking. Key management is performed through a secure key‑exchange protocol based on Elliptic‑Curve Diffie‑Hellman (ECDH) with a 256‑bit key agreement. Finally, all security controls are validated against the ISO 26262 safety lifecycle, with penetration‑testing, threat modelling, and continuous monitoring to mitigate cyber‑threats while preserving safety integrity levels.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
